interactions.summary: Jiang answers why empires miss challengers and why secret societies are powerful: hubris prevents anticipation, while ritual trauma, guilt, and fear create unstoppable motion.
A student asks about the Sabine women myth and why a conquered wife would thank the conqueror.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Student or YouTube question as voiced in class

interactions.summary: Jiang answers that the myth is a metaphor for war and that conquest silences the victim, letting winners write the soul of the defeated.

claims.claim: Empires fall because hubris makes Athens, Sparta, or any empire unable to imagine a future challenger.
Secret societies work by creating trauma, guilt, hatred, and contempt that force members to keep moving rather than rest with their guilt.
Jiang interprets the Sabine women story as a metaphor for war: kill the enemy, take the wife, then create a mythology that says she thanks you.
Conquest lets winners impose history and identity because the defeated are no longer allowed to speak or think for themselves.

signature_moments.moment: A real empire would not fall if it could genuinely imagine falling.
signature_moments.source_phrase: If an empire recognized that it could fall one day, it would never ever fall
signature_moments.why_it_matters: Captures Jiang’s hubris model of decline.

signature_moments.moment: Secret societies run on guilt that cannot stop.
signature_moments.source_phrase: they can only do more evil in order to escape the evil
signature_moments.why_it_matters: Turns secret power into trauma mechanics rather than hidden knowledge.

signature_moments.moment: Conquest is described as taking the soul, not only the body.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This is the sharpest formulation of imperial narrative control.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: Imperial arrogance that prevents imagining replacement or fall.
Groups Jiang describes as powerful through trauma and guilt-driven momentum, not necessarily secrets.
Imperial rule over the defeated person’s memory, speech, and self-understanding.
